elements.sent-2411-248: This section details the stakeholders that are involved in benchmark development and use processes.
elements.sent-2411-249: Benchmark developers are the individuals or teams who create benchmarks from scratch (e.g. BIG-Bench \cite{srivastava2023beyond ), by expanding on previously developed benchmarks (e.g. MedMNIST v2 \cite{Yang2023 ), by integrating multiple existing benchmarks (e.g. HELM \cite{liang2023holistic ), or by both expanding upon and integrating other benchmarks (e.g. Decoding Trust \cite{wang2023decodingtrust ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-250: This group’s objectives are developing benchmarks that accurately and comprehensively assess models’ capabilities or safety-critical characteristics and establishing standards for AI system evaluations that facilitate comparisons and drive progress on the specified tasks.
elements.sent-2411-251: There are three use cases for benchmark developers of our assessment, checklist, and website:.
elements.sent-2411-252: They use the checklist to understand best practices and guide their benchmark construction process pre-deployment.
elements.sent-2411-253: They use the assessment to score their benchmark after constructing it to understand any shortcomings they may address to improve the overall benchmark quality.
elements.sent-2411-254: They can use the website to find related benchmarks and compare their benchmark quality to those.
elements.sent-2411-255: Model developers are the individuals or teams who develop AI models for commercial use (e.g. GPT-4 \cite{achiam2023gpt4 ) or non-commercial purposes (e.g. Alpaca \cite{alpaca ).}}
elements.sent-2411-256: Their objectives in using benchmarks are demonstrating the performance of their models identifying areas for improvement which can guide model development and to establish credibility and encourage adoption by showcasing favorable relative performance.
elements.sent-2411-257: There are three use case for model developers of our assessment and website:.
elements.sent-2411-258: They can use the assessment results to decide which benchmarks to report.
elements.sent-2411-259: Model developers can reference our assessment results in their official reporting to indicate quality differences between benchmarks, if applicable.
elements.sent-2411-260: Model developers can use our website to find relevant benchmarks to report for their model.
elements.sent-2411-261: Model users are the individuals, organizations, or businesses which use or modify available AI models for various downstream applications (e.g. a company using ChatGPT to provide customer service).
elements.sent-2411-262: Their objective when using benchmark results is making informed decisions regarding which AI models are most suitable for their specific use cases.
elements.sent-2411-263: There are two use case for model users of our assessment and website:.
elements.sent-2411-264: If model developers don’t reference our or any similar benchmark quality assessment, model users can refer to our assessment results on the website to understand quality differences in benchmarks reported by model developers.
elements.sent-2411-265: They can also refer to our benchmark assessment results to decide between two related benchmarks who's results may both be relevant for the model user's application context.
elements.sent-2411-266: If one of these benchmarks has a higher quality, they may decide to prioritize that result based on our assessment.
elements.sent-2411-267: AI researchers are individuals or teams studying AI and related fields either at non-profits, within academic institutions, in industry, or independently.
elements.sent-2411-268: One of researchers’ objectives is using benchmarks to evaluate the performance of novel AI architectures, training techniques, and approaches, and to compare these to other systems.
elements.sent-2411-269: Additionally, they have the objective of setting research agendas based on the model limitations and weaknesses revealed by benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-270: There are two use case for AI researchers of our assessment and website:.
elements.sent-2411-271: Based on our website and assessment results, AI researchers may analyze benchmarking practices in more detail to understand challenges of benchmark developers and drive research on open questions in AI evaluations and AI benchmarking more broadly.
elements.sent-2411-272: They can use our website to understand the overall AI benchmark landscape.
elements.sent-2411-273: Regulators and standard-setting organizations may be affiliated with government agencies, international bodies, and industry associations.
elements.sent-2411-274: In these roles, they are responsible for creating and enforcing standards and regulations for AI development and use.
elements.sent-2411-275: Examples of such entities are the AI Safety Institutes, the ISO, and the EU Commission.
elements.sent-2411-276: The objective of these stakeholders is using benchmarks to assess the compliance of AI models with established regulations, guidelines and standards for traits such as performance, fairness, and safety.
elements.sent-2411-277: For example, the UK AI Safety Institute recently released their Inspect evaluation framework \cite{ukaisafety2024inspect that includes several benchmarks that we scored in our assessment, among other evaluation strategies.}
elements.sent-2411-278: Regulators and standard-setting organizations can refer to our checklist to design regulatory requirements, e.g., by only accepting benchmarks as proof for compliance by model developers that completed certain or all criteria in our checklist.
elements.sent-2411-279: They can also mandate that only benchmarks that achieved a certain score on our assessment may be used to proof compliance with regulatory requirements.
elements.sent-2411-280: During the design stage, a benchmark’s purpose, scope, and structure are defined.
elements.sent-2411-281: This requires developers to identify key aspects of an AI system that the benchmark will assess.
elements.sent-2411-282: Based on this decision, they must determine the tasks, datasets, and evaluation metrics which will be used in their benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-283: To inform these decisions, developers consider the requirements of potential users, possibly collaborating with and gathering feedback from these and other stakeholders.
elements.sent-2411-284: At this stage, the benchmark is constructed and all necessary components are aggregated.
elements.sent-2411-285: Developers collect, process, and (if applicable) annotate the datasets to be used for their tasks.
elements.sent-2411-286: They then create the evaluation scripts which allow models’ performance on this data to be measured.
elements.sent-2411-287: So that new models can be evaluated, developers may implement user interfaces and APIs which enable access to and interaction with the benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-288: This stage concludes with the initial testing and validation of benchmark components.
elements.sent-2411-289: To facilitate the benchmark’s use and interpretation, benchmark developers need to create comprehensive documentation.
elements.sent-2411-290: This includes preparing detailed descriptions of benchmark tasks, datasets, and evaluation metrics.
elements.sent-2411-291: Additionally, developers may provide instructions for how to access, use, and submit to the benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-292: Documenting design decisions, limitations, and potential biases enables stakeholders to make informed decisions regarding benchmark use.
elements.sent-2411-293: Creating resources for running the benchmark, such as quick-start guides, code documentation, and examples or tutorials is an essential step for accessibility.
elements.sent-2411-294: Once the benchmark and its documentation are released, developers must conduct regular maintenance to ensure ongoing usability.
elements.sent-2411-295: They may monitor benchmark usage and performance to identify areas for improvement and track users’ compliance with release requirements.
elements.sent-2411-296: Other tasks at this stage include addressing issues or bugs and incorporating user feedback into updates.
elements.sent-2411-297: Developers can regularly update documentation and support materials.
elements.sent-2411-298: Additionally, they can assess the continued relevance and utility of the benchmark by monitoring performance on the benchmark and responding to community feedback.
elements.sent-2411-299: The final phase of a benchmark’s lifecycle is retirement.
elements.sent-2411-300: Benchmarks are phased out or replaced when they become saturated (i.e. model performance reaches the benchmark metric’s ceiling), the task studied loses relevance, or better alternatives emerge.
elements.sent-2411-301: During retirement, developers communicate their plan to stakeholders and can provide guidance on transitioning to alternatives.
elements.sent-2411-302: They archive benchmark data, code, and documentation.
elements.sent-2411-303: As a benchmark is retired, developers may share insights gained with the AI community.
elements.sent-2411-304: Finally, they should clearly mark the benchmark as ``retired'' on channels for deployment and platforms publishing its results.

elements.sent-2411-331: To provide an overall assessment of a benchmark's design and usability, we aggregate the subscores into two key measures:.
elements.sent-2411-333: Showcases how clear about a benchmark is about its intended purpose and scope and how interpretable it is.
elements.sent-2411-334: Equivalent to the design stage subscore $s_D$.
elements.sent-2411-335: Indicates how easy the benchmark is use and how well it is documented and maintained.
elements.sent-2411-336: Weighted average of the implementation, documentation, and maintenance scores, see Equ.. 1.
elements.sent-2411-337: The discrete 0/5/10/15 point scale provides clearer differentiation between criteria that are not addressed, partially addressed, and fully addressed compared to a continuous scale.
elements.sent-2411-338: At the same time, it allows for a quantitative analysis compared to a letter grade scale like A/B/C/D.
elements.sent-2411-339: Allowing for an N/A option handles criteria that may not be applicable to certain benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-340: The 0/5/10/15 scale also allows for more granular distinctions compared to a narrower scale like 0/1/2/3 in the final scores: The difference between a score of 5 (acknowledged but not addressed) and 10 (partially addressed) is easier to see than between a 2 and 3 on a narrower scale.
elements.sent-2411-341: With a smaller range, the difference between scores is less meaningful and it is harder to separate the varying degrees of benchmark quality.
elements.sent-2411-342: Providing subscores for each lifecycle stage, while rolling them up into overall Design and Usability Scores, enables assessing benchmarks at both a category and aggregate level.

elements.sent-2411-603: Standardized documentation facilitates easier comparison between datasets and benchmarks, promotes best practices in data reporting, and helps identify potential issues or gaps in the dataset's coverage.
elements.sent-2411-605: Explanation: The evaluation metrics used are clearly specified and defined, both for standard and custom metrics tailored to the specific task or domain.
elements.sent-2411-606: The exact formulas or processes used to calculate these metrics, along with any parameters or thresholds employed, are made transparent.
elements.sent-2411-607: Justification: Documenting the evaluation metrics and scoring process is essential for enabling users to understand how the benchmark quantifies model performance and determines rankings or comparisons.
elements.sent-2411-608: By providing clear and detailed information about the metrics and scoring methods, users can assess whether the chosen metrics are appropriate for the task at hand, align with their own evaluation criteria, and provide a fair and meaningful basis for comparing different models or approaches.
elements.sent-2411-609: The exact formulas or processes used to calculate these metrics, along with any parameters or thresholds employed, are comprehensively documented.. \end{itemize \end{itemize.}}
elements.sent-2411-610: Report statistical significance of benchmark results for at least one model \begin{itemize.}
elements.sent-2411-611: Explanation: Benchmark developers run statistical significance tests on the benchmark results.
elements.sent-2411-612: They report results for, e.g., more than one random seed, and provide variance bounds.
elements.sent-2411-613: In cases where the benchmark is perfectly deterministic, this should be explicitly stated.
elements.sent-2411-614: Justification: Not doing statistical significance testing can significantly reduce the validity, utility and confidence in results \cite{biderman2024lessons .}
elements.sent-2411-615: Especially for benchmarks, we want to understand how much of the results are due to noise and how much is caused by true differences between the models tested.
elements.sent-2411-616: In cases where the benchmark is perfectly deterministic, this is explicitly stated.. \end{itemize \end{itemize.}}
elements.sent-2411-618: Explanation: The benchmark/its associated paper was accepted to a peer-reviewed journal, conference, or similar venue.
elements.sent-2411-619: Justification: Acceptance at a peer-reviewed venue signifies that the benchmark has undergone an evaluation by an external party, ensuring its validity, reliability, and scientific merit \cite{ali2016peer .}
elements.sent-2411-620: This peer review process contributes to the credibility and assurance to users that the benchmark meets established standards of quality and relevance \cite{ali2016peer .}
elements.sent-2411-622: Explanation: The benchmark developers clearly specify the applicable license for the benchmark in the code repository or paper.
elements.sent-2411-623: This includes providing information about the conditions under which the benchmark can be used, modified, and distributed.
elements.sent-2411-624: Justification: Specifying the applicable license ensures legal clarity and compliance for benchmark users and enables wider adoption, as commercial users might not be able to use the benchmark if no license is specified.
elements.sent-2411-625: Code usability checked within the last year \begin{itemize.}
elements.sent-2411-626: Explanation: The main files of the public code were updated within the last year\footnote{We recognize that this criterion is just a proxy for checking code usability, but we assume that if the main code was edited and a build status \cite{github2024buildstatus passed, that the usability was sufficiently checked.}}
elements.sent-2411-627: Justification: Over time, packages that the benchmark depends on may be updated and become incompatible with the original evaluation/benchmark code.
elements.sent-2411-628: To ensure ongoing usability, benchmark developers must check if their code can still be used at least once a year\footnote{The one-year threshold is somewhat arbitrary but out of experience of the authors, there is some transition period until which old versions can still be reliably used and are maintained, which can vary from a few months to a few years.. .}
elements.sent-2411-629: This practice ensures that users can use the benchmark without encountering and having to fix issues due to outdated dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-631: Explanation: GitHub issues are acknowledged or addressed within three months.
elements.sent-2411-632: If there are no open issues, benchmark developers would get full points.
elements.sent-2411-633: Justification: Over time, users may find issues with the benchmark tasks or implementation.
elements.sent-2411-634: To ensure continued usability, benchmark developers should address these concerns in a reasonable amount of time.
elements.sent-2411-635: Promptly responding to user feedback helps maintain the reliability and relevance of the benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-636: Alternatively, there are no open issues\footnote{This is an imperfect proxy for a maintained feedback channel.}
elements.sent-2411-637: It may be that the benchmark is working well or it may be that the benchmark is not used enough for issues to occur.
elements.sent-2411-638: However, maintenance is a critical part of benchmarks, and we hence decided to include an imperfect proxy rather than not including this criterion at all.. .
elements.sent-2411-639: Provide contact details of person responsible for benchmark \begin{itemize.}
elements.sent-2411-640: Explanation: The benchmark should include contact details of the person responsible, such as a corresponding author in the associated paper, a contact person listed on GitHub or the website, or an available online feedback form.
elements.sent-2411-641: Justification: Providing contact details ensures that users have a communication channel for inquiries, feedback, or reporting issues related to the benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-642: This transparency supports effective collaboration and resolution of problems, enhancing the benchmark's usability.
